data.title: MongoDB Q3 Earnings Preview: It's Time For A Change In Strategy
data.description:
After a spectacular Q2 earnings report a couple of months ago, the date for MongoDB's (NASDAQ:MDB) Q3 2026 report is fast approaching. The company is scheduled to report it on 1st of December, which is right after the Thanksgiving holiday weekend.

Investors' reaction to the Q2 report quickly brought MDB to new highs for the year and since then the stock has retained its gains and continued to perform roughly in-line with the market. This was in stark contrast to the reaction to the Q4 2025 report in March when the stock fell sharply.

Although the sharp jump in August was a breath of fresh air for investors, MDB is still just barely above the Nasdaq Composite index for the past 3-year period. On top of that, as we could see on the graph below, MDB share price exhibits extremely high volatility which makes quarterly earnings reactions far less impressive for long-term investors.

MongoDB's stock is also subject to very high market risk with a beta coefficient that is now above 4. Meaning that investors should expect returns of more than four times those of the equity market, in order to be compensated for market risk.

All that highlights the importance of evaluating share price performance through the lens of risks and avoid falling victim to emotional biases stemming from positive market reactions following one earnings release. With that out of the way, we could now take a closer look at what happened during the last quarter and pinpoint some key areas that investors should focus on during the upcoming release.

Profitability In The Spotlight

Since I first covered MDB in 2024 I have been highlighting the issues related to the company's GAAP profitability. Even though the last quarterly report was well-perceived by investors it did little to dispel some of these fears that MDB might have serious difficulties becoming a highly profitable entity on a GAAP basis.

The headline of the Q2 report was quite impressive with a massive beat both on the top and bottom line. This was enough to get some investors excited about the future and to ignore the significant Non-GAAP adjustments.

Management guidance for the full fiscal year was also increased from an initially expected Non-GAAP Earnings Per Share to be within the range of $2.94 to $3.12, to the newly provided range of $3.64 to $3.73 or an increase of almost 22%.

Nonetheless, the gross margin continued falling and is now at its lowest levels since 2023. The recent drops have completely offset improvements registered over the past two years. This is a concerning trend for a company that is undergoing a period of unprecedented demand for its services which usually results in higher pricing power.

Another ongoing issue for MDB is the level of Non-GAAP adjustments and their dynamic. Although Non-GAAP EPS skyrocketed and the guidance for the full fiscal year was increased, the GAAP results went in the other direction. The net loss on a GAAP basis widened during the last quarter and with that the level of Non-GAAP adjustments is now near record-high levels.

These adjustments are primarily related to stock-based compensation packages, but in Q2 2025 period we also had restructuring expenses, amortization of intangibles related to the recent acquisition of Voyage AI and income tax adjustments. That's not a positive signal that MDB is headed to achieving GAAP profitability, but quite the opposite.

The good news is that cash flow from operations improved significantly on a year-on-year basis, but due to the significant stock-based compensation expense, the gap with operating income remains high.

Even though cash flow is arguably a more useful metric for shareholders, MDB still trades at a ridiculously high cash flow multiple.

The same is true for MDB's earnings multiples on historical and forward Non-GAAP basis.

As a result, although MDB management just recently announced that they expect the company to deliver results at the high end of the previously provided guidance, the share price is down since the announcement was made.

Based on preliminary, unaudited results, the Company expects to exceed the high end of the provided third quarter fiscal year 2026 guidance for revenue, non-GAAP income from operations, and non-GAAP earnings per share, driven by continued strength in Atlas.

Source: MongoDB Website

The Management Change

During the same announcement from about a month ago, it also became clear that the CEO who took MongoDB public all the way back in 2017 will be stepping down and a replacement has been found. It is still too early to speculate on the exact reasons and the expected change under the new management, but it is highly likely for MDB to change course over the coming years.

To me personally, this sends a strong signal that the board of directors has finally recognized that a major change in strategy is needed and I would not rule out a potential M&A deal down the line.

All that once again confirms that the current strategy based on organic growth is not working and MDB's management needs to either expand more aggressively or look for a larger company to acquire it.

Source: Seeking Alpha

Another confirmation that MDB management might be working towards such a scenario came earlier this year after one of its major peers - Couchbase was acquired by a larger company. With the other major peer - ArangoDB, operating as a privately held company, this leaves MDB as the only pure-play publicly traded NoSQL database provider.

The probability that MDB would not be able to realize the necessary economies of scale in order to be highly profitable on a GAAP basis has now increased significantly and in my view this is what's going to drive the major shift in strategy.

The company has been growing very fast in recent years and expected revenue growth rate remains at around 20%, but even after all that the level of fixed cost to sales remains prohibitively high at above 80%. What's even worse is that this ratio has increased even further during the last two reports.

That is why, investors should keep a close eye on the level of fixed costs during the upcoming release. As results are anticipated to come at the high-end of the previously provided guidance, one would assume that this ratio should come down significantly in Q3.

Investor Takeaway

After reporting much better Non-GAAP results in Q2, MDB is on track of yet another strong quarter next week. While results are expected to come at the high-end of the previous guidance (or maybe even above it), a repeat of investors' reaction to the Q2 release appears less likely. Investors should also look for any clues of a potential strategy shift under the new CEO, alongside signs that Non-GAAP adjustments and the share of fixed costs to sales could come down drastically.

data.title: Kratos: Strong Quarter, But Stretched Valuation
data.description:
Thesis

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions (KTOS), Inc. is an American company specializing in the production of low-cost aircraft, mostly drones. The quarterly results were very good. A few months ago I wrote an analysis on the stock and gave it my "hold" rating.

The defense sector is a cyclical industry. Perhaps once upon a time. Today, in 2025, the narrative has changed. I'm European, and I learned this from the Americans. Investing in military defense is no longer optional. A country's sovereignty is only guaranteed if it possesses more precise and powerful weapons than its adversary. They don't necessarily have to be used, but they do act as a deterrent to enemies.

Earnings Season (Q3 2025)

I've already discussed the company's business in a previous analysis, so I won't dwell on this aspect in detail. I'm more interested in analyzing the company's recent quarterly results, which beat estimates, but something didn't quite work.

We all know it: when earnings are released, investors are ruthless. If all the numbers are positive—revenue and EBITDA are rising, guidance is rising—but even just one figure doesn't please the market, then the stock gets sold. And that's what happened to Kratos Defense.

Yet the quarterly results showed truly strong numbers. Earnings and revenues exceeded expectations, and by no small amount. Yet the next day, the stock was down about 15% from its highs.

What worked

Both revenues and EBITDA were beaten; we're talking about $347.6 million in revenues, 7.72% above estimates, and $30.8 million in EBITDA versus the estimated $25-$30 million.

Guidance also surprised on the upside, both for 2025 and the following two years; We're talking about growth of 14-15% (estimated 11-13%) for 2025, 15-20% for 2026, and even 18-23% for 2027. This trend is also confirmed by the increase in margins; in fact, for 2026 and 2027, management expects an expansion of 100 basis points in the EBITDA margin.

Growth is also being driven by Poseidon, a military hardware and systems program. It's not yet clear which components are being discussed, whether ground-based or air-based. This information is still classified, but it's estimated that the project could be worth up to $750 million.

Along with this contract, other projects are mentioned: Demons (SLCM_N), Anaconda, and Helios.

There are projects that are still premature, but that, according to Eric DeMarco, CEO of Kratos, could be worth billions by 2028. The business is driven by autonomous drones, but the company has its hands full with other projects.

What Didn't Work

From this perspective, the quarterly results are a strong buy for Kratos Defense, but there are negative aspects that are having a significant impact (and in fact, the market has penalized the stock).

The company, in fact, is burning cash and has FCF that remains weak and unreassuring. During the call with analysts, management said that cash flow will remain negative for a few more years (not before the end of 2027). Finally, margins are still very complex.

The company is suffering from sales of fixed-cost products, at least for another two years. This means that an increase in the cost of a material or the price of a subcontractor directly impacts the company's margins. This implies that at least until 2027, the company is highly sensitive to these variations.

In my previous analysis, I praised the fact that a factory had been opened in Oklahoma, but currently volumes are still low because the capacity is not yet fully utilized. Therefore, fixed costs impact the low volumes produced, once again reducing the company's margins.

And again, the market hasn't looked favorably on the fact that Kratos is bidding on billion-dollar contracts, but preparing for a tender of this caliber is enormously expensive. These non-capitalizable costs end up in the income statement, reducing the company's margins.

Valuation

From a fundamental point of view, the situation is less good than the quarterly results. Although Q3 2025 raised guidance, the stock's multiples already reflect what was promised in the investor call, and perhaps even more: the stock is already priced in for 2028.

Kratos's P/E is 142x for 2025, dropping to 68x in 2027 and 54x in 2028, despite earnings growth of 40% in 2026 and 26% in 2028. The numbers speak for themselves: Kratos has strong earnings growth, but its P/E isn't decreasing at the same rate. Therefore, the market in 2025 is paying hyper-growth multiples for 2028.

I'm talking numbers and prices now to understand whether the price at least reflects the 2028 forecast and has room for growth. The 2028 adjusted EPS is $1.39 according to the consensus. Studying competing defense companies that also deal in autonomous aircraft, the average P/E is 49x 2028 earnings. So, if I were to apply this multiple to Kratos' EOS, I would obtain a company value of $68 per share, slightly lower than the current price. From my perspective, the stock doesn't seem undervalued, but I also don't think it makes sense to increase exposure. The valuation doesn't suggest an imminent collapse, so if you already own the stock in your portfolio with a CPA below the current price, a hold seems reasonable. The stock's price already incorporates much of the defense boom and future contracts, effectively leaving little room for error. That's part of the game, and for this reason, while continuing to hold my shares, I'm maintaining a Hold rating.

Risks

First point: low margins

The stock is pricing in very high future growth. The P/E ratios tell us this, the capex tells us this, and the sales of fixed-price products tell us this. The market rewards Kratos for what it can do in the future rather than what it is already doing. This is normal; it happens with all companies, but if expectations are extremely high, it doesn't take much for it to fall.

Second point: defense trend

Kratos Defense has been driven by massive defense investments. We must be aware that if this trend were to slow, or a single project were to be delayed, the stock would move very high. And not positively.

Opportunities

It's not all negative news; the company is well-positioned, it's closing government contracts, it has a functioning division that drives the company forward, and others that are expanding in various areas.

It has a market capitalization of $12.512 million, which, for me as a company analyst, means it still has ample room for growth.

Conclusion

As I said before, I maintain my hold rating on the company. Kratos has ridden the right wave; it's a defense company at a time when there are too many wars. Military spending is very high and will continue to be significant, but there are weaknesses that cannot be ignored. The AI-powered autonomous drone sector is driving the company, and future projects certainly offer enormous potential.

The market has recently become more selective, separating companies that are cash-crunching and generating margins (currently) from those that thrive on contracts and short-term narratives (be they technology companies or, as in this case, defense companies) but are not yet solid (or are even burning cash). These types of companies have risen sharply in recent years (KTOS is +190% YTD), but I believe the market is dampening this euphoria, slowing this disproportionate growth.

I have the stock in my portfolio, and I think it makes sense to hold it, but if I were in the shoes of someone who decides to buy the stock today, perhaps I would avoid it. The stock is trading at excessively high price-earnings. Even when I bought it, the price-earnings were high, but the market context was different, the market was less demanding, and I prefer not to fall in love with companies that could be the first to be sold, precisely because they have grown too much.

Kratos Defense is a company that is capitalizing on market momentum with contracts already signed, but its margins, cash flow, and high P/E suggest to me that the market has already priced in the company's future growth.

The company is benefiting from the defense boom, but it still has some cost issues to resolve. Furthermore, it is entirely dependent on government contracts.

data.title: Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Performance Review
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary.

The Wasatch Global Value Fund—Investor Class gained 6.36% in the third quarter of 2025, outperforming the MSCI All Country (AC) World Value Index, which added 6.13%. Contributions to the Fund’s performance relative to the benchmark were led by positioning within the information technology (IT) and consumer-discretionary sectors, while positioning within the health care sector weighed most heavily on relative performance.

Leading contributors to the Fund’s performance included Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA). While the company posted better-than-expected earnings supported by growth in its cloud computing services segment after a mild disappointment in the prior quarter, this was a relatively small part of the quarter’s narrative. Alibaba has invested heavily in integrating AI across its e-commerce, logistics and cloud-computing ecosystems while developing its own large language model AI tool. The stock was initially boosted alongside the broader Chinese IT sector by news in July that NVIDIA (NVDA) would resume sales of its H20 AI chips to China. Subsequent reports that Alibaba had developed an AI processor to compete with NVIDIA’s product for the Chinese market, propelled the stock higher against the backdrop of investors’ enthusiasm for all things AI.

Pharmaceutical and medical-device company Johnson & Johnson (J&J) (JNJ) posted second quarter revenues and earnings that were better than expected. Management also increased guidance, signaling to investors conviction with respect to the long-term growth trajectory of J&J’s “innovative medicine” pharma segment. This growth is being fueled by the company’s pipeline of treatments in development as well as by acquisitions such as the $14.6 billion purchase of Intra-Cellular Therapies in January. As a result, we believe J&J is well-positioned relative to peers with respect to mitigating the impact on earnings from lost exclusivity as drugs run off patent.

Samsung Electronics Co. Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) is a leading provider of dynamic random access memory (DRAM) chips widely used in PCs and mobile phones. The stock rose on optimism that Samsung is poised to benefit from demand for the ultrafast high bandwidth memory (HBM) chip architecture used in more advanced AI applications. Specifically, in the quarter Samsung’s most advanced DRAM chip, the HBM3E 12H, qualified for NVIDIA’s graphics processing unit (GPU) platform for AI inferencing. In addition, production volumes for Samsung’s foundry business are expected to receive a boost from demand for HBM chips.

Leading detractors from Fund performance for the quarter included Centene Corp. (CNC), an insurance provider with a large Medicaid business. Under the Trump Administration, all Medicaid plans offered through exchanges set up under the Affordable Care Act have been subject to changed determination guidelines aimed at improving “program integrity,” with the effect of reducing enrollment of healthier individuals. Centene pulled its full-year guidance entering the quarter due to lack of clarity around rising utilization rates and costs associated with its Ambetter Medicaid plan, leading to a sharp decline in its stock price. While subsequently announcing disappointing earnings in late July, Centene’s management stated that the company’s Medicaid offering was underpriced for the rising morbidity of enrollees. We believe the uncertainty around pricing will subside and Centene will be able to successfully address these challenges.

Amdocs Ltd. (DOX) is an IT consulting and services company that helps clients manage complex processes and enhance the customer experience. Amdocs posted solid results in the quarter while reporting a lower volume of unfilled orders. Investors appeared to be concerned that the reduced backlog was an indication that the company’s business model was coming under threat from AI. However, we found no evidence that the reduced backlog was the result of anything other than normal variability in the timing of new business wins. Amdocs is actively engaged in helping clients utilize AI to modernize their operations. We view the company as more likely to emerge as an “AI winner” than to have its services simply displaced by AI.

Sentiment softened with respect to German insurer Munich Re as the company posted earnings that were in line with expectations but were viewed as low quality, (i.e., the result of higher-than-expected investment income and having dodged catastrophic losses rather than being driven by strong underwriting). In this vein, the company missed revenue targets and modestly lowered guidance for its core reinsurance segment. More broadly, investors have been concerned that pricing power for property and casualty insurers is undergoing a cyclical waning after a period of strength. We view the weakness as part of the normal cyclicality of the insurance business and we’re comfortable maintaining our position. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

data.title: Wasatch Global Value Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Global equity market returns were broadly positive in the third quarter of 2025, boosted by the outlook for declining U.S. interest rates and investors’ enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI).

U.S. equities added to gains seen over the first half of the year, supported by continued positive economic growth, an easing of fears over the impact of tariffs, and strong corporate earnings reports. In addition, the U.S. Federal Reserve lowered short-term rates by a quarter-point at its September meeting, a move widely interpreted as signaling the resumption of policy easing. This backdrop led the market to assume a “risk-on” tone led by the stocks of companies viewed as likely beneficiaries of spending related to AI.

Chinese stocks posted outsized returns on policy support for domestic chipmakers and accelerating spending on AI, along with easing trade tensions with the U.S. and steps by the government to address excess capacity in key industries. Japanese equities also rose notably on a trade deal with the U.S. and the outlook for central bank easing. Returns in the U.K. and Europe were also positive but more modest. German equities finished with a small gain for the quarter after hitting all-time highs earlier in the year. French stocks rose slightly despite a period of political instability.

The Wasatch Global Value Fund—Investor Class gained 6.36% in the third quarter of 2025, outperforming the MSCI All Country (AC) World Value Index, which added 6.13%. Contributions to the Fund’s performance relative to the benchmark were led by positioning within the information technology (IT) and consumer-discretionary sectors, while positioning within the health care sector weighed most heavily on relative performance.

Details of the quarter

Leading contributors to the Fund’s performance included Chinese e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding Ltd. (BABA). In addition, production volumes for Samsung’s foundry business are expected to receive a boost from demand for HBM chips.

Leading detractors from Fund performance for the quarter included Centene Corp. (CNC), an insurance provider with a large Medicaid business. While subsequently announcing disappointing earnings in late July, Centene’s management stated that the company’s Medicaid offering was underpriced for the rising morbidity of enrollees. We believe the uncertainty around pricing will subside and Centene will be able to successfully address these challenges.

Amdocs Ltd. (DOX) is an IT consulting and services company that helps clients manage complex processes and enhance the customer experience. Amdocs posted solid results in the quarter while reporting a lower volume of unfilled orders. Investors appeared to be concerned that the reduced backlog was an indication that the company’s business model was coming under threat from AI. However, we found no evidence that the reduced backlog was the result of anything other than normal variability in the timing of new business wins. Amdocs is actively engaged in helping clients utilize AI to modernize their operations. We view the company as more likely to emerge as an “AI winner” than to have its services simply displaced by AI.

Sentiment softened with respect to German insurer Munich Re as the company posted earnings that were in line with expectations but were viewed as low quality, (i.e., the result of higher-than-expected investment income and having dodged catastrophic losses rather than being driven by strong underwriting). In this vein, the company missed revenue targets and modestly lowered guidance for its core reinsurance segment. More broadly, investors have been concerned that pricing power for property and casualty insurers is undergoing a cyclical waning after a period of strength. We view the weakness as part of the normal cyclicality of the insurance business and we’re comfortable maintaining our position. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

Equity valuations remain elevated, suggesting a degree of caution is warranted. That said, global liquidity is at record levels and that money continues to seek investment avenues, advising against taking an overly defensive posture in the Fund.

Throughout 2025, we’ve been evaluating opportunities in more economically sensitive stocks with the potential to benefit from central bank rate cuts while also weighing the risks to these stocks from any tariff-driven softening in economic conditions. Over the third quarter, we added exposure on the margins to what we see as attractively valued cyclical companies with high degrees of operating leverage (i.e., mostly fixed costs, meaning that increases in revenues largely flow to bottom-line earnings).

The Fund’s overall positioning remains somewhat defensive against an evolving global trade backdrop. As such, the Fund remains tilted toward lower volatility, higher dividend areas of the market such as financials, utilities, real estate investment trusts (REITs), telecommunications and large-cap pharmaceuticals. Looking ahead, we favor international markets relative to the U.S. and Europe relative to Asia, based largely on valuations and where monetary policy is most supportive.

As always, we will continue to adhere to our stock selection discipline regardless of the short-term direction of the global markets in response to a shifting macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

data.title: SharpLink Gaming: How To Get Ethereum Exposure At A 17% Discount
data.description:
On October 1, I published an article titled "Why SharpLink Gaming Is My #1 Ethereum Bet Right Now," and I gave the stock a Buy rating because it was a good Ethereum treasury company trading near its mNAV, and the long-term tailwinds for Ethereum were (and still are) worth betting on. Well, SBET has fallen by 41.5% since that rating, and Ethereum USD (ETH-USD) has fallen quite a bit as well. But to my defense, I did warn that Ethereum is no stranger to volatility and that investors should wait for a dip before getting more aggressive.

As I write these words on November 25, the price of Ethereum sits at $3,014 per token. Meanwhile, SharpLink Gaming, Inc. (SBET), whose business model is to accumulate Ether and stake it, trades at 0.83x its fully diluted mNAV. The company holds $2.6 billion worth of ETH but has an enterprise value of $2.167 billion. In other words, this is like buying Ethereum at a 17% discount, or a price of around $2,500.

SharpLink gaming is fundamentally sound and, so far, hasn't issued any debt and preferred shares in the same way that Strategy Inc. (MSTR) has done to buy Bitcoin USD (BTC-USD). This makes it much less risky, making the discount to mNAV more attractive. Due to its discount to NAV (which can reverse in a bull market), relatively low risk profile for a treasury company, and Ethereum's long-term tailwinds, I remain bullish on SBET stock for the long term.

A Quick Note To Clear Any Confusion

If you're someone who read my last two articles, which were on Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) and Alexandria Real Estate Equities, Inc. (ARE), you'll notice that I gave them both a Hold rating besides their valuations being low. My reasoning was that a low valuation isn't always enough to justify a Buy rating. So, why am I giving SBET a Buy rating mainly due to its low valuation? It may seem inconsistent, so I just want to clear up any possible confusion.

The first reason is that SBET is much easier to objectively call undervalued, at least relative to its ETH holdings, so investors may not ignore that valuation discount forever. Another reason is that this is specifically a long-term Buy rating. I'm taking a multi-year approach here. Third, I'm still warning investors about being super aggressive because buying into downtrends can be risky without some sort of confirmation of a bottom (more on that later). Lastly, I just find the catalysts for ETH to be more impressive than those of Novo Nordisk and Alexandria Real Estate. With that said, let's get to the analysis.

What Happened To SBET Stock?

I'm sure some investors are concerned about the continuously falling share price of SBET stock recently. But if you were an Ethereum bull before and are still an Ethereum bull now, then you're good, because nothing major has changed with SharpLink Gaming. The only thing that changed was crypto sentiment.

The Sentiment Change

On October 1, the CMC Crypto Fear and Greed Index was at 51, or neutral. Now, it's at 18, which is in the extreme fear range. I don't expect SBET stock to be trading at a premium during "extreme fear" moments, but I also don't expect the market to be in a perpetual state of extreme fear. Sentiment always bounces back.

A Valuation Collapse Despite Solid Fundamentals

When I last wrote about SBET, the stock was trading at 1.02x its fully diluted mNAV, which was close to a "fair" valuation since it was basically trading in line with the value of its ETH holdings. The company also held 838,728 ETH at that time, and it now holds 862,029 ETH, while its ETH concentration has ticked up slightly from 3.99 to 4.01.

A quick way to calculate ETH concentration is to divide the number of ETH that SBET holds by each 1,000 "Assumed Diluted Shares Outstanding," according to SharpLink. The good news is that the company calculates it for us on its ETH dashboard. The higher, the better.

The company still has no debt and still owns just Ethereum. It also continues to earn staking rewards from its fully staked ETH holdings. From November 16 to November 2023, its staking rewards rose from 7,403 ETH to 7,846 ETH, a 443 ETH increase. At an ETH price of $3,014, that's around $1.3 million worth of ETH staking rewards in a week. In the past 8 reported weeks, its staking rewards grew from 4,272 ETH to the most recent 7,846 ETH, or an average of 446.75 ETH per week.

I understand why a company like SBET would fall to around a 1x premium (or slightly lower) during rough times, but to be honest, I don't see any good reason for the stock to be trading at 0.83x mNAV, which is quite a noticeable discount.

A Quick Look At SBET's Q3 Results

SharpLink Gaming released its Q3 results on November 12. Let's go over the results. Net income was $104.3 million, or $0.62 per fully diluted share compared to a loss of $885K (-$3.02 per diluted share) in the same period last year. That's due to ETH share price gains, although the company is now down around $432 million on its ETH investment.

Next, SBET's revenue grew by over 1,100% to $10.8 million due to the change in business model from being an affiliate marketing company (which is now its legacy business) to an ETH treasury. The affiliate marketing revenue was just under $570K. The remainder of the revenue came from staking.

Staking Revenue Should Continue Rising

As long as the price of ETH doesn't crash, I expect staking revenue to increase over time. The more ETH SharpLink gets (whether from staking rewards or ETH purchases), the more it can stake, and the more it will earn.

Staking revenue doesn't show up on the cash flow statement as cash flow, so if you take a look at free cash flow, you'll see that it was a negative $6.3 million for Q3. However, the staking rewards SBET receives should outpace the cash outflows. I mean, SBET generated $1.3 million worth of ETH in staking rewards in just one week, and a quarter has 13 weeks. I'm aware that the numbers will fluctuate week to week, as they depend on multiple factors, but just to simplify it, 13 x $1.3 million will get you $16.9 million in a quarter.

My point is that the company could theoretically sell some of its ETH that it earns from staking to cover costs if it ever needed to, while not sacrificing all the staking rewards. The staking rewards should also cover stock-based compensation if it remains low, as it was just $3.6 million in Q3.

SharpLink's Financial Position Is Strong At The Moment

SharpLink ended the quarter with $11.1 million in cash and $26.7 million in USDC stablecoins, so we can say that its cash and cash equivalents were $37.8 million. Meanwhile, SBET has no debt.

The good thing about SharpLink Gaming having no debt or preferred securities is that Ethereum can crash 80% from here, and we wouldn't have to worry about the company becoming insolvent. We also wouldn't have to worry about the company issuing shares to meet its obligations (SharpLink even stated on its ETH dashboard that it will "never utilize its At-the-Market (“ATM”) facility while trading below NAV").

We'd just have…other issues at that point, mainly with the share price. Conversely, a company like Strategy has a bunch of periodic payments it needs to make, which become harder to make if the price of BTC crashes.

The Long-Term Bull Case For Ethereum Is Still Intact

I already mentioned the bull case for Ethereum in my ETH article linked in my introduction paragraph, as well as in my last SBET article. It has only been 2 months since then, so I don't think the thesis has changed. First, stablecoin usage is still growing (and most stablecoins are circulating on Ethereum). As of November 25, the total Ethereum stablecoin supply was $182.3 billion. That's up from $174.8 billion on October 1, when my last SBET article got published.

Second, tokenization is still a big catalyst. As I stated in my ETH article, tokenization refers to taking assets like Treasuries, corporate bonds, real estate, and more and creating digital versions of them on the blockchain, with the Ethereum network having a large market share of tokenized assets under management.

The Risks

The main risk here is the price of ETH itself, as recent investors have learned. We're now 39% off the all-time high, and the peak-to-trough drop was about 47%. But as I've stated in past articles, ETH is no stranger to 50-80% drawdowns, so there's no guarantee that this is the bottom. Right now, we're experiencing a multi-day bounce in a multi-week drop. It could very well be a "dead-cat bounce." It's too early to say. But at least the extreme fear gives us hope that we'll soon be due for a sentiment reversal.

Another risk is the potential for the company to raise debt. I'm not saying that any debt raised is automatically bad, as it can be done in an accretive fashion, but the company needs to be smart about it and not reckless. Debt raises are not off the table for SBET, as management said the following in the Q3 earnings call:

When our multiple to NAV is below 1, we can raise capital to fund share buybacks. We can do this by monetizing our volatility through convertible bonds or other equity-linked structures, and we can utilize a portion of the $3 billion of ETH on our balance sheet as collateral to borrow capital.

This is something to monitor going forward.

The Bottom Line On SharpLink Gaming Stock

Now that sentiment has turned negative around crypto and ETH treasury companies, there are relative bargains to be had. At the moment, you can purchase SBET stock at a fully diluted mNAV of 0.83x. If you're a long-term Ethereum bull, this is a chance to pick up ETH at a discount. Buying SBET at this point can lead to an outperformance relative to the price of ETH because it's very likely that when/if ETH-USD eventually recovers, SBET's multiple will recover as well, and it can likely head well over 1x mNAV in times of greed.

This doesn't include the fact that the company can issue shares at a premium (during optimistic times) and buy back shares under mNAV during rough times, all to increase ETH per share. I discussed this in more detail in my previous article.

Certainly, this will be a volatile investment, but I believe Ethereum's long-term upward trajectory will continue in the long term, and SBET is a great way to bet on it at a discount.

data.title: Royal Caribbean Is A Growth Model Through Scale And Megaships.
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This isn't my first approach to valuing Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL), but it's the first time I've formed a rating and understood the company's business as a conservative investor. My coverage begins with a moderate hold rating and a 4% undervaluation relative to my fair value calculated using historical multiples.The company stands out for its high profitability and manageable debt for the industry, but comparing expected growth rates based on the quantitative approach with actual growth rates has contributed to the modest final rating.

Thesis Disclaimer

I use a hold rating, but my approach would be better served by a rating scale from avoid to attractive. Neutral status or hold rating relative to RCL stock is based solely on an analysis of the financial performance of the business without an assessment of the current value, although this analysis will also be carried out towards the end of the article. My opinion may be useful to investors considering whether to include the stock in a long-term portfolio and hold it for a 3-5 year horizon, and it does not constitute a recommendation or trading instruction.

Thesis Explanation

My latest article was dedicated to Royal Caribbean's competitor, Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH). While I don't like comparative analysis, I will occasionally resort to it, either unwittingly or for illustration purposes, even though RCL was analyzed separately, but on the same points as its competitor.

And while NCLH, due to low margins and a significant debt burden, scored only 35 points out of 100 on my quantitative system, Royal Caribbean surpassed it by as much as 17 points, scoring 52/100, and therefore has a higher rating - hold.

I'm a quantitative analyst. To determine whether a company's stock is attractive for investment, I compare eight financial metrics with benchmarks I've established to subjectively define outstanding businesses. These metrics inform the overall ranking I provided above.

Looking at Royal Caribbean's business quantitatively, I don't see any obvious weaknesses. Given its strong financial performance, its significant debt doesn't seem like a big deal, especially since it's typical for companies in this industry. However, the key element behind my relatively low rating is the high base effect. What do I mean by this? It appears the company will struggle to maintain the current growth rates it has achieved since the pandemic, which could lead to a revaluation of the market's current perception of the company.

Stably high margins and ambiguous growth prospects lead me to the core idea of ​​my thesis: hold if the stock is in your portfolio and buy at a significant discount if you don't. That's my strategy.

Outstanding Company In Terms Of Business Model.

In my quantitative investment approach, I believe that high profitability reflects strong competitive advantages. RCL's net profit margin for Q3 2025 exceeded 23%, and its ROIC of 15.2% is quite high for the industry. This can be explained by the distinctive features of the company's business model.

Launched in 2024, Icon of the Seas is not just the largest cruise ship in the world; it embodies the essence of the entire corporation's business approach. The Icon class is the competitive edge that differentiates RCL's business from its competitors. Thanks to its exceptional onboard revenue it delivers a disproportionately high RevPAX (Revenue Per Available Guest-Spot).

While not targeting the upper premium segment, the company is a leader in the so-called mass premium market: mass market + premium. Its focus on megaliners maximizes onboard revenue through a vast array of entertainment options, leading RCL to the following business model: operating leverage through scale.

To support this description, the company reports a load factor of 112% in Q3 2025. In a previous article about NCLH, I noted its significant success, with the company achieving a load factor of nearly 106%. This confirms that RCL's model is working, as it means that often three passengers purchase tickets for a two-berth cabin.

I usually write about debt separately. But assessing the debt burden is the most insignificant piece of the puzzle. Moreover, it directly impacts the business's marginality. RCL is larger in scale than NCLH ($17.4b versus $9.7b in revenue), but its total debt of $21b is only 35% higher than its peer's. Ultimately, using the average level over the past 12 months, I conclude that RCL's interest coverage is 4.73, which is twice as high as Norwegian Cruise Line's and close to the ideal benchmark of 5. Furthermore, interest expenses TTM are deducted somewhat less from total revenue, which also impacts overall marginality.

Slightly Below Fair Value

Valuation, in my paradigm, is important when I've decided that adding a stock to my portfolio makes sense for business reasons. Since RCL scored 52 out of 100 according to my system, I consider it a stock I can buy, carefully and preferably at a discount.

But first, my standard exercise of calculating fair value using the historical multiples method. I collect data on quarterly EPS and the closing price of the reporting day. Fortunately, Seeking Alpha provides the necessary data depth.

After suffering losses in 2020-2022 due to the pandemic, the company was able to recover and reach double its pre-COVID operating profit. To calculate fair value, I'll use the market P/E valuation starting in Q2 2023, mitigating potential outliers with a tool called the median.

The median of the entire sample gives me a price-to-earnings ratio of ~19, corresponding to a price of ~$276 per share, which is 3.6% higher than the closing price on Nov 25. I call this level "fair value by historical multiples." The medians of the upper and lower thirds of the sample provide two additional levels: $336 per share represents excessive optimism and an overpremium in the market for me, while $236 per share represents the TTM minimum valuation over the past 10 quarters. This is the price range I'd use to include RCL in my portfolio, taking into account its moderate rating.

Expected Growth Is The Reason For The Modest Rating

Despite my admiration for the financial performance metrics, the overall rating of 52 out of 100 on my scale is considered moderate, and the main reason for this is the growth metrics.

48.8%. This is the 3-year CAGR of net profit I calculated. It's impressive. However, this is already priced. Based on analyst forecasts, EPS by the end of 2027 could be $20.45, meaning earnings will grow at a rate of 16.6% over the next two years. That's three times less. The numbers are still in double digits, but the growth rate is declining significantly, so I factored this into the final rating as a significant risk.

The second point is also related to the numbers. To assess the sustainability of business growth, I use a custom metric called the Stability Ratio. To calculate it, I divide the average YoY growth by the standard deviation of the sample over the period. Starting in the second half of 2024, the company achieved fairly consistent growth. However, as you can imagine, the recent pandemic prevents us from calling the medium- or long-term growth sustainable.The Stability Ratio for the last 12 quarters is 0.6, compared to my benchmark of 1.

If we take the last seven years, the value of this ratio is practically zero: with an average YoY growth of 8%, the standard deviation of the sample is almost 150%. This indicates a high level of uncertainty in Royal Caribbean's profit generation track. In other words, the pandemic happened, so why can't something similar happen again?This is another factor why I lowered the overall rating, ultimately arriving at a modest 52/100, pending a price discount for inclusion in the portfolio.

Two Points Why I Might Be Wrong In My Moderate Rating

Royal Caribbean is actively expanding its fleet, focusing on "Gigaships"—a powerful driver of business growth. Despite my modest quantitative growth estimate, the company is currently perceived as experiencing "quality growth" rather than recovery.

Strategic partner Meyer Turku has received an order for the fourth Icon, scheduled for launch in 2027. Options for Icon 5 and 6 have been announced. According to Cruise Industry News, a total of 8 new ships will be added to the RCL group portfolio over the next 5 years.

But RCL isn't just interested in its ships. This year, the company launched two exclusive private resorts for its clients: Labadee on the northern coast of Haiti and CocoCay, a private island in the Bahamas. In essence, this is a vertically integrated "water amusement park": the margins for island visits are significantly higher than those of a typical day at sea. These assets further strengthen RCL's already competitive margins.

The company plans to open five more similar destinations in the next two years. Outside the traditional Caribbean, notable locations include the Royal Beach Club Santorini in the Mediterranean and The Cormorant in Chile, which is slated to become the World's Southernmost Hotel.

I'd venture to guess that if all launches and construction go according to plan, and the world frees us from unpleasant force majeure, RCL could easily surprise analysts with its earnings per share in 2027-2028.

Risks To The Thesis

My comprehensive rating likely takes into account some business risks, which is why I use it. However, beyond the rating, I would highlight industry risk. The 2020 pandemic exposed the problems of some industries, and while we don't want anything like that to happen again, it can't be ruled out. We now know that this isn't just a risk worth highlighting, but a real and significant one. Add in various geopolitical factors, and we get a rather risky investment proposition purchasing RCL shares. And since RCL uses megaships as leverage, the suspension of any one of them would result in greater losses than for any other company in the industry. This uncontrollable risk suggests that, if RCL is included in a long-term portfolio, it should be a small one.

Bottom Line

For me, Royal Caribbean is a clear and transparent idea, with its limitations and prospects. The company's business model allows for strong financial metrics, and debt management simultaneously allows for fleet expansion without harming shareholder returns. However, it's safe to say that buying RCL shares in 2025 isn't as attractive as it was immediately after the pandemic. Since the market has already priced in the current growth rate, and future growth is expected to be more modest, I'll wait for a price around $235 to re-evaluate the idea of ​​acquiring a good business with a discount.

data.title: Celcuity: From Speculative Bet To Pre-Commercial Powerhouse
data.description:
In February, I covered Celcuity (CELC), stating that the stock price was fundamentally discounting its risk/reward ratio. By that time, Celcuity was a speculative play with deep skepticism around the toxicities associated with oral PI3K inhibitors and a balance sheet that, although strong, suffered from the same high rate of cash burn typical of late-stage oncology clinical trials. My central premise was that if gedatolisib could prove effective without the metabolic toxicity seen in the other oral PI3K inhibitors, then the Celcuity stock would likely undergo a major re-rating. Nine months later, Celcuity's situation is dramatically different; The speculative thesis has mostly proven true through what management described as "unprecedented" Phase 3 results, the completion of a New Drug Application, and a large capital investment to address short-term funding concerns.

VIKTORIA-1 Data Validates the Dual Inhibition Thesis

In my last coverage, I stated that Celcuity has taken a unique approach by inhibiting both PI3K and mTOR through intravenous administration in order to sidestep the toxicities associated with oral PI3K inhibitors such as alpelisib, including hyperglycemia and liver toxicity, as well as many of the resistance mechanisms commonly observed with oral PI3K inhibitors. The recent clinical data from the PIK3CA wild-type subgroup of the Phase 3 VIKTORIA-1 trial, which was recently published and presented orally at the ESMO congress as a late breaking abstract, provides strong evidence to support this hypothesis.

Not only did these results show statistical significance, but they appear to be clinically meaningful for patients with HR+/HER2- advanced breast cancer who have failed prior CDK4/6 inhibitor-based therapies. The efficacy data shown in this trial are unprecedented for this patient population. The combination of gedatolisib, fulvestrant, and palbociclib, known as the gedatolisib triplet, had a Hazard Ratio of 0.24 when compared to the control arm. Therefore, this combination provided a 76% reduction in the risk of developing recurrent disease or dying compared to fulvestrant alone. In comparison to the control arm, the median PFS of 9.3 months for the gedatolisib triplet represents a 7.3-month increase in PFS compared to the median PFS of 2.0 months for the control arm. Notably, even the gedatolisib doublet, which lacked palbociclib, had a Hazard Ratio of 0.33 and a median PFS of 7.4 months. Based upon the efficacy data, this medication appears to provide a substantial advantage to the standard of care for this patient population. Furthermore, based upon the PFS, patients treated with this medication can expect nearly a 400% longer duration of time without experiencing recurrence of their disease compared to those patients receiving the standard of care.

Perhaps most importantly, the safety profile of this medication, the primary concern I identified in my February article regarding the potential for severe metabolic disturbances to limit its use, was acceptable in the Phase 3 trial setting. As I previously mentioned, severe metabolic disturbances are common in this class of medications, leading to severe warning labels that severely limit the number of prescriptions written for them. In VIKTORIA-1, the incidence of Grade 3 hyperglycemia was found to be less than 3%, or 2.3% of the patients in the triplet arm. This low incidence of Grade 3 hyperglycemia suggests that Celcuity's intravenous formulation and dual inhibition mechanism have been successful in eliminating one of the class's most serious side effects. Although neutropenia occurred frequently in the triplet arm, with an incidence of 52.3%, the rate of discontinuation due to treatment-related AEs was very low, at 2.3%. The fact that the discontinuation rate was so low is arguably the most commercially relevant metric, since it implies that oncologists will be able to manage the side effects and continue treating patients long enough to experience the survival benefits.

Regulatory Milestones and the Path to Commercialization

The execution has been rapid since the data readout. On November 17, 2025, Celcuity reported that it had completed the submission of its New Drug Application (NDA) to the FDA. The NDA was submitted under the Real-Time Oncology Review (RTOR) program, which is an important signifier; drugs approved through the RTOR program are typically expected to provide substantial advantages over current therapies, and as such can be reviewed by the FDA in real-time with interaction with the drug developer, rather than through a standard, linear review process. The acceptance of Celcuity's NDA into the RTOR program validates the strength of the data from VIKTORIA-1 and may indicate a faster regulatory path to approval than is common for new molecular entities.

In addition, management has indicated that the results of the trial have the potential to be practice changing based on the large magnitude of improvement shown in the progression free survival data. Since the submission of the NDA is complete, Celcuity is transitioning from being primarily focused on the clinic to conducting pre-commercial operations. This transition is evident in Celcuity's hiring activity. In Q3 2025, Celcuity's operating expense increased significantly due to several factors, including a $3.2M increase in operating expense specifically related to the addition of commercial personnel and other launch related activities. These actions clearly indicate that Celcuity's management is preparing the marketplace for a rapid launch following the potential approval of VIKTORIA and is establishing the necessary infrastructure to support the rapid commercialization of the product following approval.

Financial Fortification Removes the Dilution Overhang

While Celcuity has a lot of money in the bank, we all know that biotechnology companies can burn through that money at an alarming rate, creating dilutive equity that could hurt the value of shares to the very shareholders who are looking for the stock to take off when it finally does. That's what happened -- although instead of being a death blow, this new money appears to have greatly reduced the risk for Celcuity's potential long-term success, as opposed to being an opportunity cost.

Celcuity went ahead and completed a simultaneous public offering of convertible notes, common stock, and pre-funded warrants in July of 2025, raising $287 million. They also increased the amount of money available under their existing debt facility to a total of $500 million. And they were given the option to draw down an additional $30 million of the $500 million if the positive VIKTORIA-1 study results are achieved (which they did).

At the end of the third quarter of 2025, Celcuity's balance sheet showed it to have cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments totaling $455.0 million, representing a significant increase from the $264.1 million in the same three-month period last year. Based upon management projections, they believe they will have enough cash to support their business until at least the start of the commercialization phase of their product.

This substantial influx of capital has essentially eliminated the short-term financing risks that plague so many biotechs during the regulatory approval process. Rather than having to scramble to find another source of capital, the Company will not need to go back to the markets for funding for quite some time - their "war chest" is fully funded.

That being said, investors should understand that there are increasing costs associated with the transition into the commercial space. For example, Celcuity's operating expense for the third quarter of 2025 was $42.8 million, a significant increase over the $30.1 million in operating expense recorded in the comparable three-month period of the previous year. Research and Development expense alone increased to $34.9 million. While the cash burn is certainly greater, the company still has a large cash position of $455 million and can continue to operate at this level for approximately 10 quarters before generating revenue.

Pipeline Expansion Beyond the First Win

The success of the PIK3CA wild-type cohort will certainly get attention from investors. However, there are other drivers in the pipeline that were speculative in my last article but will provide actual data in the future. Data from the VIKTORIA-1 trial for the PIK3CA mutant cohort will come in the spring of 2026 when the trial is completed. Since gedatolisib acts through a mechanism that targets the pathway independent of whether there is a mutation, as opposed to those drugs that target specific mutations, the confidence level should be higher due to the positive data seen in the PIK3CA wild-type cohort. If gedatolisib is working so well in patients with no mutation, then the mechanism supports the idea that gedatolisib should work just as effectively in patients with mutations, if not even better since the pathway is overexpressed.

Additionally, the Company is rapidly advancing into the first-line treatment arena. The first patient in the Phase 3 VIKTORIA-2 trial was initiated in July 2025. This trial is evaluating the efficacy of gedatolisib when combined with a CDK4/6 inhibitor and fulvestrant in patients with hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative advanced or metastatic breast cancer that has progressed on prior endocrine therapy. A successful outcome in this trial will expand the total addressable market by several fold compared to what is being targeted in VIKTORIA-1.

Conclusion and Future Outlook

In my previous article, I referred to the prostate cancer program as an underappreciated value driver. More recent updated Phase 1b data from Celcuity that was presented at ESMO has started to support this viewpoint. In patients with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC) that were treated with the combination of gedatolisib and darolutamide, the median radiographic progression-free survival was 9.1 months. What is even more impressive, the median rPFS in the patients treated with the lower dose of gedatolisib at 120 mg was 9.5 months. These data indicate that gedatolisib has efficacy in a second major disease area and therefore represents the opportunity for long-term revenue diversification outside of breast cancer.

Celcuity's risks are evolving from science to business execution. While the likelihood of the product failing in clinical trials (a binary risk) has been significantly decreased based upon the VIKTORIA-1 results for the wild-type population, the major risks today relate to potential regulatory surprises, although the RTOR status will help mitigate this, as well as the operational challenges associated with the successful commercialization of a new product. As the R&D and administrative costs continue to increase as Celcuity prepares for the launch of its lead product, it must be able to properly control its burn rate against the approximately $455 million in cash available to ensure profitable operations. In my prior article, I described Celcuity as an "all or nothing proposition." Today, with a New Drug Application filed, a defined safety advantage confirmed, and sufficient funding established through at least 2027, it appears the "all" part of the equation is being executed. Celcuity has successfully transitioned from a speculative biotechnology company to a pre-commercial firm with a de-risked lead product.

data.title: Forget Alphabet's TPU, AI Distribution Is Unrivaled With Cloud And Search
data.description:
Meta (META) engaging with Alphabet's (GOOGL) TPUs was big headline news. Many headlines implied that Nvidia shares declined due to fears that TPU would take market share from its GPUs.

So Alphabet (GOOGL) already looks like an AI winner on the surface, but I believe the more interesting story for investors is underneath: a high-margin ads and subscription machine that is quietly adding a second profit pillar in Google Cloud while using AI to deepen the moat around Search, YouTube, and Workspace.

Google's Business Today And Core Investment Thesis

Alphabet reported about $350 billion in revenue and $112 billion in operating income in 2024, for an operating margin a bit above 32%. Google Services (Search, YouTube, Network, subscriptions, devices) contributed roughly $305 billion of revenue and over $121 billion of operating income, while Google Cloud added $43 billion of revenue and a little over $6 billion of operating income. Around three quarters of Alphabet's revenue still comes from advertising.

In Q3 2025, Alphabet delivered just over $102 billion of revenue, up 16% year over year, with double-digit growth across Search, YouTube, subscriptions and Cloud. Google Services revenue grew about 14% to $87 billion, while Google Cloud revenue grew 34% to $15.2 billion, with management highlighting AI as a key driver.

Alphabet is one of a very small number of companies that can do three things at once: manufacture AI compute at scale through its data centers and TPU chips, develop frontier models in-house through Gemini and DeepMind, and distribute AI-native experiences through products used by billions of people every day. Management notes that several AI Overviews now serve well over two billion monthly active users, and the Gemini app alone has reached more than 650 million monthly active users.

Google Cloud And The Full-Stack AI Platform

Google Cloud has become Alphabet's primary way to monetize the AI boom directly. By Q3 2025 it had $15.2 billion in revenues, up 34% year over year.

What makes that growth durable is the nature of the workloads. AI applications are compute and data intensive, very sticky once deployed, and increasingly tied into proprietary models and data pipelines. Google Cloud's stack runs from custom TPUs and GPUs, through Gemini models and Vertex AI, to managed data platforms and security, and finally into application layers like Workspace and industry-specific solutions. For customers, moving an AI workload off this stack is not trivial. Each new AI project tends to pull in additional storage, networking, and higher-value sales.

Just look at the backlog, stated in the Q3 earnings:

We continue to drive strong growth in new businesses. Google Cloud accelerated, ending the quarter with $155 billion in backlog

The economic profile of Cloud is being transformed at the same time. The segment went from an operating loss in 2022 to over $6 billion of operating income in 2024, and recent quarters show operating margins above 20% as utilization improves and higher-margin services grow as a share of the mix. If revenue continues to grow in the high-teens to ~20% range over the next three to five years, it is reasonable to expect Cloud margins to settle in the mid-20s. On those assumptions, Cloud could reach around 20% of Alphabet's revenue and a much larger share of incremental operating income.

There is also upside optionality in Alphabet's AI hardware. The previously mentioned fact that Meta has been in talks to spend billions on Google's TPUs indicate they are potentially a differentiated product Google can sell externally. That dynamic reinforces the "full-stack" nature of Google Cloud's AI offering.

Personally, I think it is best to wait and see before calling it one way or another on TPUs. I know Nvidia enjoys a substantial ecosystem moat because of CUDA. Nvidia chips are supported in basically all AI data centers and the network effect is strong. TPUs depend on the Google stack, and so far, they've been only in larger bilateral deals. However, options are very important, and I see the TPU optionality as just additional factors that make the stock more attractive, beyond the core thesis. I see Waymo in pretty much the same light, too.

Put together, Cloud is shifting from being a "nice adjunct" to Google Services into a genuine second profit engine. In a base-case scenario, I see Cloud delivering high-teens revenue growth and margin expansion over the next several years, contributing meaningfully to mid-teens EPS growth at the Alphabet level even if Search growth slows modestly from today's pace.

AI-Native Search, YouTube, And Subscriptions

Besides Cloud's great results, Alphabet is also deploying AI into its own consumer and enterprise products at global scale. This is the segment called "Google Services."

In Search, Google has rolled out AI Overviews and an AI Mode experience that synthesize information directly in the results page and keep users inside Google's surfaces. Ads and commercial units are already integrated into these AI answers, and Google has been explicit that it sees AI features as expanding total search usage rather than cannibalizing it. Q3 commentary and other coverage point to higher engagement and query growth in markets where AI Overviews are enabled, not a collapse in traditional click-through behavior.

This is the core of Alphabet's AI distribution advantage. Rivals like OpenAI and Anthropic can build strong models or apps, but Google via Search still controls the main discovery surface for a huge portion of the Internet. If AI Overviews, shopping units, and vertical experiences keep users inside Google's ecosystem, then AI simply becomes a richer interface for the same searching and buying behavior that already supports a massive ad business.

YouTube is a great frontier for this. In 2024, YouTube ads generated about $36 billion of revenue, and when you include subscriptions like YouTube Premium and YouTube TV, the total YouTube business was over $50 billion. In Q3 2025, YouTube ad revenue grew around 15% year over year to roughly $10.2 billion, with strong contributions from both Shorts and connected-TV formats.

Shorts is still under-monetized relative to longer video, and CTV ad loads remain below linear TV norms. AI helps on multiple fronts with ads. It can make better recommendations and be more brand aware, more effective targeting, and an expanding suite of creator tools like editing, dubbing, and video generation. I think these dynamics support a path where YouTube can grow revenue at 10-15% for several years, with improving margins as AI tools scale and ad products mature.

Layered on top of Google Gemini, access to consumers at monthly price points ranging from around $20 to roughly $250 with large storage and additional perks.

Services is a rapidly growing subscriptions and productivity stack. Workspace bundles Gemini-powered features into business and enterprise plans, while Google AI Pro and AI Ultra bring premium AI services.

This model matters for two reasons. First, AI features can help raise ARPU and reduce churn across both consumer and enterprise products. If everything is all in one place, there is no need to switch, and the customer is more likely to spend more money on Google if they want more out of Services.

Second, by embedding Gemini into core workflows (Gmail, Docs, Meet, Sheets, Slides, etc) Google can gradually displace a long tail of SaaS solutions in writing, note-taking, task management, meeting transcription, productivity, and more. I'm thinking features of Notion, Zoom, Trello, Asana, Slack might be emulated by the Google stack. Over time, that should add the revenues of many other such businesses to Google Services's top line.

My base case view over the next three to five years is that Alphabet can grow consolidated revenue at least 10% annually, with modest operating margin expansion as Cloud and subscriptions mix up. In a bear case where Search growth drops to low single digits, YouTube grows ~5%, and Cloud slows to ~10%, Alphabet still likely delivers high-single-digit growth with operating margins settling in the high-20s. Those are still excellent numbers for a company of this scale.

Risks

I think the main risks cluster around execution, competition, regulation, and infrastructure.

On AI execution and competitive dynamics, Alphabet faces intense pressure from Microsoft and OpenAI, from Anthropic's Claude and xAI's Grok, and from open-source models that make advanced AI broadly accessible. Google Cloud remains third in global cloud infrastructure share at roughly 13%, behind AWS and Azure, even though it is gaining ground. If competitors out-innovate Google in agents, productivity tools, or model quality, the revenue and margin trajectory I outlined above may prove too optimistic.

There is also some regulatory risk. In the U.S. search antitrust case, the court concluded that Google is a monopolist in general search and search advertising and imposed behavioral remedies around distribution and data sharing, stopping short of a breakup but still constraining future contracts and data flows. In the EU, the Digital Markets Act is forcing changes to how Google links services like Search, Maps, YouTube, and Chrome, with ongoing investigations into whether Google's implementation is compliant. Such regulations could weigh on Search economics over time.

There is also risk around the broader publisher and content ecosystem, though this isn't specific to Google. AI Overviews and other generative features can reduce click through to websites, prompting pressure for licensing schemes or revenue sharing that could increase content costs or constrain how aggressively Google can roll out AI-heavy experiences. But again, this likely affects other AI competitors.

Infrastructure and power constraints are another emerging issue. Google executives have talked internally about needing to double AI serving capacity roughly every six months for several years to meet demand. If the power grid or supply chains cannot keep up, or if the expected AI revenue does not materialize quickly enough, returns on this capex wave could disappoint.

Finally, suppose AI for whatever reason over the next few years is not able to justify the recent capex. This could really sour sentiment and compress multiples in all tech names. This thesis, of course, assumes that AI is not a bubble and that it will unlock significant productivity and value creation on the backend.

Conclusion

Given my base case of roughly 10% revenue growth and mid-teens EPS growth supports a four-year market-cap target of about $6.8 trillion, assuming a mid-20s earnings multiple (GOOGL just crossed 31x earnings) holds roughly steady as Cloud and subscriptions scale and the company continues to buy back 1-2% of shares per year. From a current market cap of $3.9 trillion, that implies roughly 75% upside over four years. In a bear case where revenue growth slows toward 5% and the multiple compresses more, I would expect a flattish to only modestly higher market cap over the same horizon, with most of the return coming from buybacks rather than multiple expansion.

The main developments that would push me away from a Buy rating are: sustained Search share loss, solid evidence that AI features are not enhancing ad economics, or a regulatory outcome that permanently pushes margins well below expectations.

When we add the possibilities of TPU sales and Waymo, it creates meaningful additional upside to this base case. This article was focused on Alphabet's edge in AI distribution, but these other factors can lead to substantial value outside of what I covered here. To cap this part off, I think you can buy GOOGL for just the AI distribution story that leverages its Services and Cloud business moats, and you'd also be getting TPU and Waymo for free.

I rate GOOGL a Buy.

data.title: Columbia Select Large Cap Equity Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market overview

U.S. equities moved higher during the third quarter, building on gains achieved in the first six months of the year. Sentiment was supported by continued positive economic growth, receding fears about the impact of tariffs and robust second-quarter corporate earnings. In addition, the U.S. Federal Reserve lowered its benchmark overnight lending rate by a quarter point at its September meeting, its first rate cut of 2025, and investors began to anticipate that further easing was in store. This backdrop fueled a surge in investor risk appetite, leading to a rally highlighted by stocks viewed as beneficiaries of spending related to artificial intelligence (AI).

The growth style comfortably outperformed value, thanks to continued strength in the information technology sector. Enthusiasm about the growth potential of AI remained a key driver of outperformance for the tech sector. Still, the third quarter was characterized by broad participation beyond mega-cap technology stocks.

Quarterly portfolio recap of performance relative to the benchmark

In sector terms, selection within financials led positive contributions to relative performance, followed by communication services and utilities, while selection within information technology weighed most heavily on performance, followed by health care and consumer discretionary.

Contributors were highlighted by overweight exposure to large banks within financials, including Citigroup (C) and Goldman Sachs (GS). Sentiment around these stocks benefited from an improving regulatory outlook, which should support continued capital returns to shareholders, along with a pickup in capital markets activity, a strong trading environment and healthy net interest income.

Selection within communication services and utilities contributed modestly to relative performance. Within utilities, contributors were led by Xcel Energy (XEL), which rallied after it settled litigation around a fire in Colorado on more favorable terms than expected. With the settlement behind it, the company is better positioned to deploy capital, suggesting the potential for strong returns going forward.

Elsewhere, while overall selection lagged within information technology, electrical components manufacturer TE Connectivity (TEL) was among the top individual contributors. The company primarily targets the industrial and automotive markets and is well positioned to benefit as AI drives robotic and automation initiatives.

On the downside, within information technology (IT), On Semiconductor provided weaker-than-expected guidance on its recovery in several end markets, most notably autos. Shares of enterprise productivity software company ServiceNow (NOW) also came under pressure, due largely to concerns around corporate IT spending being redirected toward AI initiatives, as well as fears that new AI productivity software will disrupt incumbent providers. We view the company as positioned to benefit over the long term as it deploys AI in its products. Shares of Synopsys (SNPS), a provider of automation and design tools to semiconductor manufacturers, dipped sharply as the market reacted unfavorably to an acquisition and weakness in the China market related to tariffs. In addition, the company noted reduced purchases by one of its large customers. The stock slid as investors downgraded the company’s outlook; however, we remain positive on the long-term demand trends for automation and design tool companies, and we believe AI will be a tailwind to demand for those services.

Within health care, sentiment with respect to Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) suffered during the quarter, as macroeconomic concerns and hospital budget constraints contributed to slower-than-expected early sales growth for the company’s newest robotic surgical device. We view the slow ramp-up of sales and softer margins as temporary issues not related to the company’s execution, and we expect international markets and new procedures growth to support Intuitive Surgical’s growth trajectory. Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) was another laggard within health care, despite posting strong results. Uncertainties around its pipeline of treatments and efficacy of a new pain drug weighed on the stock.

Within consumer discretionary, athletic shoe and apparel designer and marketer Nike (NKE) underperformed. Investors remained skeptical about Nike’s overhaul of its product development and sales strategy, despite a return to revenue growth in key markets. In addition, the market was concerned about the impact of tariffs and declining China revenues on Nike’s profitability.

Finally, exposure to beverage marketer Constellation Brands (STZ) within consumer discretionary was a leading individual detractor. Management lowered guidance with respect to beer sales, citing pressure on the company’s core consumer base and overall category weakness.

Outlook

We remain constructive on the outlook for equities, based on improving business confidence, a renewed Fed easing cycle, and economic tailwinds to capital spending from the recently passed budget bill. Signs of a recovery in lending and capital markets activity further support the outlook. Risks around labor market softening and the impact of tariffs remain, but appear manageable in the context of broader economic resilience, especially if inflation remains contained.

More broadly, while seeking to construct a portfolio that can be resilient to a range of economic outcomes, we continue to focus on stock selection as the driver of returns. In so doing, we seek a combination of certain characteristics that we believe has the potential to outperform throughout a market cycle. We emphasize companies with strong free-cash-flow generation, improving revenue and earnings trends, high or rising returns on invested capital and sound or improving balance sheets.
